channel.title: The Intelligence
channel.description: The Economist — The Intelligence — The Intelligence

item.title: Crude awakening: Iran oil shock
item.description: As America and Israel continue to bombard Iran, much of Iran’s retaliation is directed against energy infrastructure . With tankers blocked and oil…

item.title: Escalation: Middle East war widens
item.description: The war in Iran has entered its fourth day with further American and Israeli strikes, and Iranian retaliation across the region. Now Israel’s prime…

item.title: War with Iran: Middle East in flames
item.description: This weekend, America and Israel launched long-anticipated attacks on Iran , killing Ali Khamenei, the country’s supreme leader. Our correspondents…

item.title: Bot the difference: AI’s absence in economic data
item.description: For all the promise of transformation that artificial intelligence offers, a close look at macroeconomic data shows little change . Sit tight. A…

item.title: Poised and confused: the will-he-won’t-he of Iran strikes
item.description: As a military build-up continues in the Middle East, President Donald Trump’s messaging remains ambiguous . What could, at this stage, head off…

item.title: Chapo, Mayo, Mencho: another Mexican kingpin falls
item.description: The targeting of “El Mencho”, the leader of one of the country’s two biggest gangs, has resulted in a predictable spate of violence —more of which…

item.title: A world-changing war: four years in Ukraine
item.description: On the fourth anniversary of a war that many predicted would last mere days, much has changed—even beyond the unthinkable misery in Ukraine itself .…

item.title: When the levy brakes: Trump’s tariffs struck down
item.description: The smackdown by America’s Supreme Court was resounding : the bulk of Donald Trump’s tariffs were instituted illegally. He will try to rebuild his…

item.title: The arrest is history: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor
item.description: The detainment of King Charles’s brother is almost without precedent in Britain’s long royal history. He denies wrongdoing, but damage to “The Firm”…

item.title: The splitting image: Yoon verdict will deepen divisions
item.description: Yoon Suk Yeol, South Korea’s ex-president, has been handed a life sentence for insurrection. That is by no means the end of the story of division in…

item.title: The Robin Hood state: taxes are getting more progressive
item.description: Income inequality abounds and today’s rich are staggeringly rich, implying that tax regimes are giving the wealthy more and more of a pass. Our dig…

item.title: Ice, ice, maybe: should the Arctic be refrozen?
item.description: Many scientists are considering the notion of actively cooling the region that is warming fastest. We examine the merits and the risks—both…

item.title: Check in the mail: our analysis of Epstein’s correspondence
item.description: Our data journalists trawled through the vast email archive of Jeffrey Epstein, a dead sex offender. It is a revealing look at how and with whom he…

item.title: Stock options: how to hedge an AI bubble
item.description: Tech firms are spending so much on artificial intelligence that investors are getting nervous. Our correspondent explains whether it is possible to…

item.title: Check in Kyiv: prospects for peace?
item.description: As Russia’s war in Ukraine nears four years, there has been no let up in the fighting on the battlefield. Yet there is some optimism that…

item.title: Dhaka matters: an election for Bangladesh
item.description: The toppling of authoritarian leader Sheikh Hassina in Bangladesh in 2024 was celebrated as a triumph for democracy. Tomorrow the country finally…

item.title: A Keir-death experience: Britain's PM clings on
item.description: Sir Keir Starmer has faced calls for his resignation from a senior party member. He has survived – but Britain’s prime minister is now fighting for…

item.title: Snap judgement: Japan PM’s electoral landslide
item.description: Takaichi Sanae ’s gamble to call a general election has paid off. How will the prime minister’s thumping victory change Japan? New legislation in…

item.title: Elon shot: will Musk’s mega-merger work?
item.description: This week Elon Musk announced the merger of two of his companies: SpaceX and xAI, which makes chatbots. Is the new firm viable? As migrant workers…

item.title: Nukes of hazard: US-Russia arms treaty expires
item.description: The New START nuclear deal was signed in 2010 to restrict the number of strategic warheads and missiles America and Russia could amass. Will there…

item.title: Peter and the wolves: Mandelson falls but Epstein scandal spreads
item.description: Peter Mandelson was a totem of Britain’s Labour party for decades. The newest Epstein files mark the end of his political career. What are the…

item.title: Tug of Warsh: will the new chair politicise the Fed?
item.description: After months of speculation, Donald Trump has picked Kevin Warsh to run the Federal Reserve. Our correspondent explains what this means for…

item.title: Survival Modi: Indian PM’s fortunes revive
item.description: After Narendra Modi ’s setback in the 2024 Indian elections, many thought his star was falling. Our correspondent explains the surprising resurgence…

item.title: Democracy on ICE? The mood turns in America
item.description: On the streets of Minneapolis, in polls and in the halls of Congress, disapproval of President Donald Trump’s immigration agenda is mounting.…

item.title: No middle ground: Iran’s dangerous division
item.description: As the smoke of a murderous crackdown clears, Iranians have hardened into two camps. Moderates and reformists are out; a sense of looming civil war…

item.title: We’ll be right over here: Europe’s populists sour on Trump
item.description: Many of the continent’s populist-right leaders once saw President Donald Trump as an example-setting fellow traveller. But his actions and policies…

item.title: General ejection: China’s military purge
item.description: The two men under investigation are in the army’s highest echelon, and are some of President Xi Jinping’s closest counsel. We examine the probable…

item.title: Resistance is fatal: another killing in Minneapolis
item.description: For the second time this month, federal agents in Minneapolis killed a citizen under disputed circumstances . We take a wider look at the…

item.title: Trailer: Boss Class Season 3
item.description: AI is changing how we work. It's turning us all into managers. Be a good one. The Economist ’s management columnist, Andrew Palmer, takes on the…

item.title: ISIS control: Syria’s prison camp changes hands
item.description: Our Middle East correspondent reports from the largest holding camp for ISIS fighters in northern Syria , which government troops have just retaken…

item.title: Grave new world: the Greenland row’s lasting damage
item.description: After an astonishing week, Donald Trump has said America will not take Greenland by force, nor put tariffs on those who oppose his acquisition plan.…

item.title: House of Kurds: on the ground in northern Syria
item.description: In recent days Syrian government troops have tried to retake territory held by the country’s Kurdish minority. Our correspondent reports from the…

item.title: Power ballot: Japanese PM’s electoral gamble
item.description: Japan’s prime minister Takaichi Sanae has called an election three months into her term. Can she capitalise on her popularity, or will her…

item.title: Thin ice: could the Greenland clash kill NATO?
item.description: Donald Trump has promised to impose a 10% tariff on eight countries, all NATO members, that oppose his plan to acquire Greenland . As the argument…

item.title: Clutching at shahs: Iran’s would-be revolutionary
item.description: Reza Pahlavi, son of the shah deposed in the 1979 revolution, tells us he sees himself as the people’s choice to lead. We ask how that might work.…

item.title: Maria-view mirror: asking Venezuelans what they want now
item.description: As Maria Corina Machado, Venezuela’s opposition leader, meets with President Donald Trump, we talk through our exclusive polling on what the country…

item.title: Independence Jay? Inflation and attacks on the Fed
item.description: More inflation numbers, more jabs by President Donald Trump at Jay Powell, the Federal Reserve chairman. We ask what the Fed is doing, should be…

item.title: Lowering the steaks: a Mercosur deal at last
item.description: From Argentinian beef to German cars, a freshly inked deal between the EU and a bloc of South American countries should ease trade barriers—and is a…

item.title: Strike fear: Iran’s protests and Israel
item.description: Each country fears an attack from the other: Iran may wish to distract from internal conflict, Israel to exploit it. In an interview with Prime…

item.title: Shah caller: Iran’s protests are different this time
item.description: Demonstrations are growing once again—but this time the message is notably different, and the regime has little means available to calm tensions.…

item.title: Oil pressure: America’s tanker seizures
item.description: It had real dramatic elements: a (slow) chase scene, faked locations, a literal false flag, a daring helicopter descent. But what is the broader…

item.title: In the line of fires: LA’s ashen suburbs one year on
item.description: We return to the Los Angeles suburbs that were consumed by wildfires a year ago. The varying rebuilding efforts reveal divides in wealth, but also…

item.title: Xi’s not there: what China’s Venezuela response reveals
item.description: The countries have a decades-long relationship—debt freely given in one direction and plenty of oil sold in the other. Yet America’s intervention…

item.title: Nicolás caged: what next for Venezuela?
item.description: America’s National Security Strategy, released a month ago, suggested the administration’s focus was on dominance of the western hemisphere. But no…

item.title: State of the art: Chinese market flounders
item.description: Property wealth in China turbo-charged investment in art . Now house prices have crashed, art sales may follow. Are Britons really leaving the…

item.title: What in the world: predictions for 2026
item.description: The editor of our annual “ World Ahead ” publication predicts the themes and events that will dominate the headlines in 2026, from geopolitics to…

item.title: Years ending: notable lives lost in 2025
item.description: Today’s show is a tribute to those whose lives we remembered this year. From Pope Francis , the most open-minded pontiff for decades, and…

item.title: Boom with a view: our economy of the year
item.description: Announcing the return of The Economist’s annual ranking for best performing economy . Are you ready for the big reveal? Dalit cuisine is barely…

item.title: Generals’ selection: Myanmar’s sham poll
item.description: Five years after seizing power in a coup, the military junta in Myanmar is holding an election. Yet all credible opposition has been banned. And war…

item.title: Battle of the texts: which books changed the world?
item.description: So many books are published each year; few stand the test of time. Today we devote our whole show to asking which works have shaped the way we…

item.title: So this is quizmas: our inaugural holiday face-off
item.description: Join our editors and correspondents in a gripping test of recall and reflexes. There are questions on business and politics of course—but also news…

item.title: Growing pains: a recession in recessions
item.description: Recessions are, in their way, bad news. But so, paradoxically, is a lasting dearth of them. We explain the dangers that lie beneath the current run…

item.title: Slop stars: why AI-generated content could help creators
item.description: In a world of infinite content, who wins and who loses? Our correspondent explains what the proliferation of AI-generated art means for human…

item.title: Country of the year: The Economist’s pick for 2025
item.description: Each year, The Economist tries to identify which country has improved the most, whether economically, politically or in other ways. In a turbulent…

item.title: Weight boss? Competition for Novo Nordisk
item.description: Since Novo Nordisk launched Wegovy in 2021, it has dominated the fast-growing market for slimming drugs. Now a new jab is eating into the Danish…

item.title: Emissions possible: EU petrol ban quashed
item.description: The European Union had promised to ban the sale of new diesel and petrol vehicles by 2035, as part of its environmental ambitions. Yesterday it…

item.title: After Bondi: how to tackle extremism
item.description: As the Australian authorities continue their investigation into Sunday’s deadly attack on a Hannukah party in Sydney, investigators have uncovered a…

item.title: Judgment day: Jimmy Lai convicted
item.description: Today Hong Kong’s most prominent media mogul was convicted of flouting national security legislation. Our correspondent explains the consequences…

item.title: Right, here, waiting: Europe’s populists on the rise
item.description: In Britain, Germany and France, populist-right leaders and parties are making hay . What unites their movements, and how do their respective…

item.title: Ven and the art of hemispheric maintenance: America’s national-security posture
item.description: America’s seizure of a Venezuelan oil tanker fits with the stated goals in its new national-security strategy : untrammelled hemispheric dominance.…

item.title: Digital stimulation: AI and porn
item.description: At every technological revolution, the industry of indecency is close at hand. We look at how sex workers and porn-peddlers are making use of AI.…

item.title: “You’re…fired?” A momentous Supreme Court case
item.description: Of all the sackings at federal level President Donald Trump has carried out—and that the Supreme Court has upheld—the one now under consideration…

item.title: Transitional injustice: Syria one year after Assad
item.description: A year after ousting its despot, things are not as bad as many had feared. But old sectarian divides threaten the peace . Forced labour, sex tourism…

item.title: Keir in the headlights: interviewing Britain’s PM
item.description: The Economist’s editor-in-chief Zanny Minton Beddoes met Sir Keir Starmer for “ The Insider ”, our new video offering. We bring you the…

item.title: Delhi-novela: Putin and Modi rekindle bromance
item.description: As Vladimir Putin begins a two-day visit to India, our correspondent explains why Donald Trump’s policies have pushed India and Russia closer…

item.title: Stars and strikes: was America’s ship-bomb illegal?
item.description: America’s attacks on possible drug boats in the Caribbean is already controversial. Now critics are questioning the legality of one particular…

item.title: The great wheel: China’s Robotaxi revolution
item.description: Once derided as a copycat nation, China is now leading the world in innovation , from driverless cars to pharmaceuticals. Our correspondent explains…

item.title: Deal them back in? What we heard in Iran
item.description: Our correspondents get a feel for today’s Tehran: no morality police but still much fear of speaking out. And the foreign minister indicates a…

item.title: Fire, then fury: Hong Kong’s deadly blaze
item.description: The dead are still being found; the displaced huddle in public spaces. Who or what will be blamed, and what policies will change after the tragedy ?…

item.title: Growth negligence: Britain’s budget
item.description: The tax-and-spend plan was fine-tuned to avoid immediate political jeopardy. But it will do little to help Britain’s chronic growth problem, and is…

item.title: Party likes it 1959: Cuba in crisis
item.description: The country’s Communist Party leadership continues to cling to old ideals amid on-again, off-again diplomacy with America—and the people’s suffering…

item.title: Wage against the machine: the distortions of minimum pay
item.description: For decades governments have found minimum-wage rises a politically expedient means of redistribution. But the onward economic distortions have at…

item.title: Emission creep: a contentious COP closes
item.description: It is telling and troubling that the annual climate talking-shop’s outcome did not even mention fossil fuels. We ask whether the COP process is…

item.title: Flee country: Britain seeks to slash migration
item.description: Britain’s home secretary Shabana Mahmood proposed a big shift in immigration policy this week. Our correspondent explains Labour’s reforms…

item.title: War graft: scandal engulfs Ukraine
item.description: Pentagon officials are meeting President Volodymyr Zelensky in Kyiv to discuss a Russia-US peace proposal Ukraine had no part in writing. That…

item.title: Crown prince arming: Trump sells jets to MBS
item.description: Muhammad bin Salman’s first visit to the White House in seven years earned the Saudi crown prince new weapons, giant tech deals and a burnished…

item.title: After Sheikh: what next for Bangladesh?
item.description: Sheikh Hasina, Bangladesh’s former prime minister, has been sentenced to death for  crimes against humanity. The country is at a pivotal moment…

item.title: Gang-buster: Can Sheinbaum beat Mexico crime?
item.description: This weekend, thousands of people protested in Mexico City about violent crime. But our correspondent notes that the government has had some success…

item.title: Toil and rubble: who will rebuild Gaza?
item.description: After two years of war, over 80% of the buildings in Gaza have been destroyed. Our correspondent assesses various plans for reconstruction . Do…

item.title: Opening punch: Shutdown ends, now more Epstein emails
item.description: Last night Donald Trump finally managed to end the longest federal government shutdown in history. But, amid a new release of emails relating to…

item.title: The little green look: China’s energy revolution
item.description: America has boycotted this week’s COP climate talks, but China has sent a giant delegation. The world’s biggest polluter is increasingly pivotal to…

item.title: Home alone: the relationship recession
item.description: People are spurning marriage and any other kind of romantic relationship in record numbers. Our correspondents explore the non-dating market. The…

item.title: British Broadcasting Capitulation: BBC bosses fall
item.description: The BBC’s director-general and head of news have resigned amid accusations of institutional bias. Can the broadcaster recover its reputation?…

item.title: Heir Jordan: the rising star of France’s populist right
item.description: Jordan Bardella, the leader of the National Rally party, has a stonking lead in voting-intention polls. His plans , our correspondent says, would…

item.title: Recall of duty? Trump’s tariffs in court
item.description: Just as soon as President Donald Trump started applying sweeping tariffs on trading partners, legal challenges to them started piling up. We listen…

item.title: Into the blue: Democrats win big
item.description: In the first electoral test of Donald Trump’s second term, Democrat supporters voted in a socialist mayor of New York and governors to New York and…

item.title: Getting their ships together: America in the Caribbean
item.description: As America sends its biggest naval hardware to the Caribbean, we ask whether the intent is more than mere sabre-rattling —and why the Trump…

item.title: Capital gained: a grim turn in Darfur
item.description: The paramilitary Rapid Support Forces have at last taken El Fasher, the capital of Darfur. Reported atrocities are sharply rising , in a chilling…

item.title: Truce story: (a sketch of) a Trump-Xi trade deal
item.description: Presidents Donald Trump and Xi Jinping agreed to holster their trade weapons for now. But even if the deal holds, it does not address the deeper…

item.title: Manhattan transfer: New York’s finance folk flee
item.description: Taxes upon taxes are just one of the reasons that both financial-industry hotshots and businesses are moving out of the Big Apple. We look at what…

item.title: War-chest X-ray: how to finance Ukraine
item.description: Europe is edging closer to using seized Russian assets to finance Ukraine. The country badly needs that 140bn-euro windfall—and much, much more. A…

item.title: Bust a vessel: NATO v dark fleets
item.description: Ships of unknown origin or intent are flummoxing NATO forces on the high seas. Our correspondent visits the Latvian navy on the Virsaitis as it…

item.title: Let Milei this out: Argentina’s midterms surprise
item.description: Voters overwhelmingly backed the party of President Javier Milei, handing him a lifeline he was not expected to get. We ask how he can finish his…

item.title: The great brawl: China is winning the trade war
item.description: This weekend American and Chinese officials are meeting to try and ease tensions over trade after China’s recent decision to restrict rare-earth…

item.title: Shutting match: what will break the US federal impasse?
item.description: The government shutdown in America is now the second-longest on record. Yet there is no apparent urgency to end it, either from Republicans or…

item.title: Cocaine and able: drug runners innovate
item.description: America has been blowing up ships in foreign waters suspected of carrying drugs. That will do little to dent today’s narco-business , which is more…

item.title: Rock and rule: Japan’s first female PM
item.description: Takaichi Sanae , Japan’s new prime minister, rode motorbikes and plays heavy-metal drums. Will her political ambitions be as bold? America, a nation…

item.title: Voices of Gaza: life amid a fragile peace
item.description: Ten days into the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas, the truce remains tenuous. Today our correspondent speaks to people on the ground in Gaza…

item.title: Bolt-on charges: Trump's former adviser is indicted
item.description: John Bolton, a former Trump ally-turned-critic has been charged with mishandling classified information. That raises further fears that the justice…

item.title: Rome run: Meloni’s surprising stamina
item.description: When Giorgia Meloni was elected Italy’s prime minister, many people feared she would prove divisive and volatile. Instead, at a time when many other…
